title: Cumulative number of objects launched into space
subtitle: This includes satellites, probes, landers, crewed spacecrafts, and space station flight elements launched into Earth orbit or beyond.
caption:
Cumulative number of objects launched into space
This includes satellites, probes, landers, crewed spacecrafts, and space station flight elements launched into Earth orbit or beyond.

The line chart tracks the total accumulation of space objects launched from 1957 through 2025, with the vertical axis measuring the count of items up to 25,000. The World total begins at zero in 1957 and rises steadily to approximately 5,000 by the year 2000, then accelerates to roughly 9,000 by 2010, before curving upward sharply to reach 25,000 by the end of the timeline. The United States line follows a similar trajectory but at a lower magnitude, reaching about 2,000 by 2000 and climbing steeply after 2010 to finish near 16,000. Russia maintains a steady, linear increase throughout the period, starting early and reaching just under 4,000 by 2025. China remains flat near zero until roughly 2005, then rises to approximately 2,000 by the final year. The United Kingdom line stays low and flat for decades before rising to just over 1,000. Japan, France, and India all show gradual increases, with Japan reaching roughly 1,000, France near 800, and India around 500. Germany and the European Space Agency lines remain at the bottom of the chart, showing minimal accumulation compared to the leading nations. The visual pattern shows a distinct acceleration in launch activity globally after 2010, with the World and United States lines exhibiting the steepest upward slopes in the final decade.
